title: Heliotropium eichwaldii
intro: Heliotropium eichwaldii, also known as Eichwald's heliotrope, is a perennial plant belonging to the borage family (Boraginaceae) that is native to the Caucasus region. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities, which may help alleviate symptoms of respiratory issues and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Heliotropium eichwaldii include alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. Traditional herbal preparations made with Heliotropium eichwaldii include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat respiratory conditions, such as bronchitis and asthma, and skin issues, such as eczema and acne.
taxonomy: Heliotropium eichwaldii is a species of flowering plant in the borage family (Boraginaceae). It belongs to the genus Heliotropium and is native to the Caucasus region. This plant is further classified as a member of the Heliotropium section, specifically within the group known as 'H. subg. Heliotropium'. The classification places it as a part of the Boraginaceae family, within the order Borales. Heliotropium eichwaldii's classification reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in this family.

It is traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, coughs, and respiratory problems. side_effects: Heliotropium eichwaldii, a plant native to the Caucasus region, may pose some risks if ingested due to its toxic properties. It contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids, which can cause liver damage and other adverse effects if consumed in large amounts. These alkaloids can also affect the heart, causing issues such as palpitations and arrhythmias. Furthermore, long-term exposure to pyrrolizidine alkaloids may lead to fibrosis in the liver and other organs. It is essential to handle the plant with caution and avoid ingesting any of its parts.
